displayTitle: 2025-10-21 - Social Services and Community Committee - Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Effectiveness of arrangements for reducing child poverty
topic: Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Effectiveness of arrangements for reducing child poverty
description: 22 October 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Effectiveness of arrangements for reducing child poverty Hearing of Evidence Ministry of Social Development - Simon MacPherson, Deputy Chief Executive Policy - Hannah Kerr, Director Child Wellbeing and Poverty Reduction - Bryan Perry, Principal Advisor Strategy, Investment and Organisational Performance - Sacha O’Dea, Deputy Chief Executive Strategy and Insights Social Investment Agency - Andrew Coster, Secretary for Social Investment - Aphra Green, DCE, System Performance and Investment Advice
descriptionSummary: Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Effectiveness of arrangements for reducing child poverty. Ministry of Social Development. Simon MacPherson, Deputy Chief Executive Policy

So there've been some quite significant changes made, I think in the last year. Anything you wanna add? Um, I think the action plan is the key thing. But, um, I think at the point that the report was finalized, we were developing the action plan and the approach to, um, reporting to ministers on that. But the, the, the key actions that, um, that, that sort of organize themselves under the three priorities, including reducing material hardship. So, um, there's a range of actions sitting with MSD with the Ministry of Education, with Housing and Urban Development that, that broadly speaking, reflect, um, both the government's focus on addressing the, the sort of longer term drivers of, um, uh, of material hardship, but also addressing, uh, the immediate, um, the immediate needs.

So different ways to, um, support families with the costs that they're facing. Um, and then that is reported on quarterly to ministers, um, with a focus on identifying, um, anything that is off track that ministers need to, um, engage with to unblock and so that they can sort of see how those things are tracking.

And that would also be the opportunity if there were key dependencies between actions, we would absolutely flag that in that reporting. Um, and so, um, that, that, um, process, uh, was put in place after the report Was finalized. Um, um, and, um, look, I recall, um, how challenging it was to come up with those measures and those targets at the time of the initial, uh, legislation.

And I, and I can hear, um, how much, um, intense work goes on and to collecting that data and then, you know, feeding that through to policy development. So I, I guess, um, is the investment in time, effort, energy, um, ha having payback at the end of the day? That's a, a really big question. Um, sorry, I guess it'd answer that in, um, several ways, which might only be a partial answer. Um, and I'll get to your outcomes question at the end.

So I think, um, obviously set stature responsibilities on are hugely important topic. So, um, I think everyone's invested in doing that work and delivering the reporting to the best of our ability. So, uh, and then I think quite a lot of this work was being done anyway. So Brian's been working in this field for more than 20 years, and, um, I think stats have put some effort into improving the surveys, and that's actually a lot of the basic, uh, work was being done anyway through process that Brian was responsible for.

Um, I think if you look at the trajectory over the last 15 years in terms of child poverty, you see a, um, quite distinct trend downwards, um, most, for the most part across the three, uh, target measures, um, from the end of the GFC. So when we had employment rates at, well, unemployment rates, well over 6%, so you said a decline across all of those, uh, which is really positive, um, until about an affliction point shortly after COVID about 21, 22, when it's some things gone the wrong direction.

So I think you can actually measure it and see quite a significant impact. And that reflects a range of things, not just policy choices the government's made, but actually how the economy is feared and how employment's going. So I think, um, it gives you a, and just finally, I, because of the, um, again, reminds you around mind because of the, um, margins of error in the reporting.

It's actually important to still look at the trend rather than just a point by point change, I think. What does this mean for children in, um, house low income households? Um, the role of the SIA has been, uh, a evolving and we are now a central agency with a role to lead change in the public sector, uh, particularly, uh, in relation to how data and evidence are used to improve social outcomes, uh, from the way we invest funds.

Um, that's a reasonably broad remit. Uh, we're a small agency, uh, and we have areas of focus which I'll, I'll come onto, uh, shortly just to note that, uh, child poverty connects with, uh, a range of other disadvantage, which are areas where we are particularly focused. Um, so whilst social investment is not directly focused on child poverty, it does, um, touch on population groups who will feature significantly in child poverty statistics.

Um, probably the key document, uh, for us to reference in this context is the child news strategy, uh, which calls out, uh, a number of areas of focus or priority areas for investment, um, those being to reduce child material hardship, uh, supporting children and their families in whanau in the first 2000 days, uh, which is dear to our heart in terms of, uh, moving investment earlier in order to prevent harm, uh, preventing child harm and addressing drivers of long-term disadvantage.

So how does social investment, uh, touch on these things? Well, firstly, ensuring that data and evidence underpin investment decisions. Uh, we have a wealth of, uh, data available sometimes too much. Um, it's our job to make sure that it is used to, uh, inform decisions, uh, that resources are directed to where they can have the greatest impact.

Um, government invests a huge amount. Not all of it is deployed to things that will make the best difference. Uh, investments focus on outcomes for people rather than outputs. Um, you'll be very well familiar with our system of accountability, um, which is driven out of the reforms of the eighties and nineties. It can see us pretty focused on activity, uh, and not focused enough on the results of that activity.

And social investment is aimed at trying to, to shift that, um, ensuring that communities are able to, um, input and the shape of, to the shape of services that delivered so that they meet local needs. Um, ensuring that providers have stability, uh, that they need and the flexibility to be able to deliver services that meet the needs of the people they work with.

Um, ensuring that we're prioritizing early intervention prevention and innovation, uh, and ensuring, uh, particularly the sort of high and complex end of need that services are responsive to the individual. Um, and what will make the greatest difference. As I've said, we, we know that there's a, a, a coalescence between, um, drivers of long-term disadvantage and child poverty.

Uh, we primarily work in the IDI, uh, and that, uh, has been noted, doesn't have all of the, uh, inputs to the child poverty measures. Um, but what we are confident about is that the disadvantage that we're particularly focused on, um, does significantly aligned. The social investment fund is one of the, uh, key things that we are focused on at the moment.

Uh, it is about, um, demonstrating, enabling, uh, portfolio neutral investment that addresses as early as possible the causes of, uh, disadvantage and poor social outcomes. Um, for the funds first round of investments, we have identified three, uh, cohorts, uh, where there will be significant overlap with, um, child material hardship.

Uh, the cohorts are children of parents, um, with parents in prison, uh, children of parents who have experienced the care system and children who are stood down or suspended by the age of 12. You know, as you can imagine, these are are young people who have, um, a lot stacked against them. Uh, and we are really focused on making investments that will try and shift the dial for, uh, young people in that category very quickly.

In terms of our role, um, as I've said, you're a central agency. Uh, we have a role to look across the social sector, um, that's in the vicinity of 70 or $80 billion worth of spend, um, necessarily we have to focus in particular areas. Uh, there's obviously a lot of delivery that's very focused around individual agency accountabilities and functions. providing cross system advice, uh, generating insights and tools that will enable better decision making, um, advising ministers on investments, particularly those to be delivered through the fund, um, and making sure we've got the necessary infrastructure around data and evidence to enable social investment.
